name: Palenque Astronaut Lid
description: The 'Palenque Astronaut Lid' refers to a stone carving on the sarcophagus lid of K'inich Janaab' Pakal, a Mayan king who ruled Palenque in the 7th century CE. Proponents of ancient astronaut theories interpret the carving as depicting Pakal piloting a spacecraft. They point to supposed control panels, exhaust ports, and a breathing apparatus within the depiction. This interpretation gained traction in the mid-20th century, popularized by Erich von Däniken's book 'Chariots of the Gods?'. Mainstream Mayanist scholars, however, interpret the carving as a representation of Pakal's death and rebirth, aligning with Mayan cosmology. The 'craft' is seen as a symbolic representation of the Mayan World Tree, connecting the underworld, earthly realm, and heavens. The 'controls' are identified as symbols of rulership and power, and the 'breathing apparatus' as maize roots, symbolizing Pakal's descent into the underworld to be reborn like the maize god. The theory highlights the subjective nature of interpreting ancient art and the influence of modern perspectives on understanding historical artifacts.
subtitle: Ancient Astronaut Theory Applied to Mayan Art

claims.claim: The carving depicts Pakal piloting a spacecraft.
claims.evidence: Similarity to modern spacecraft designs, perceived control panels and exhaust ports in the carving.
claims.criticism: Mayan iconography and cosmology provide a coherent alternative interpretation: Pakal's descent into the underworld and rebirth, symbolized by the World Tree.

claims.claim: The 'breathing apparatus' represents life support systems.
claims.evidence: The object resembles a modern respirator or oxygen mask.
claims.criticism: The object is more likely maize roots, a common symbol in Mayan art representing life, fertility, and rebirth, connecting Pakal to the maize god.

claims.claim: The carving provides evidence of ancient extraterrestrial contact.
claims.evidence: The advanced nature of the supposed spacecraft suggests influence beyond human capabilities at the time.
claims.criticism: There is no other corroborating evidence of extraterrestrial contact in Mayan culture or archaeology. The interpretation relies on selectively interpreting the carving while ignoring the broader context of Mayan art and beliefs.

keyFigures.name: Erich von Däniken
keyFigures.role: Popularized the ancient astronaut theory, including the Palenque Astronaut Lid interpretation.

keyFigures.name: Linda Schele
keyFigures.role: Leading Mayanist scholar who provided detailed interpretations of Mayan iconography and epigraphy, offering alternative explanations for the carving.

keyFigures.name: Peter Tompkins
keyFigures.role: Author who popularized the Palenque Astronaut interpretation in 'Secrets of the Great Pyramid'.

timeline.event: Reign of K'inich Janaab' Pakal and creation of the sarcophagus lid.
Erich von Däniken publishes 'Chariots of the Gods?', popularizing the Palenque Astronaut theory.
Continued debate and discussion surrounding the interpretation of the Palenque sarcophagus lid.

sources.author: Linda Schele and David Freidel
sources.work: A Forest of Kings: The Untold Story of the Ancient Maya
sources.title: Linda Schele and David Freidel

sources.author: Robert J. Sharer and Loa P. Traxler
sources.title: Robert J. Sharer and Loa P. Traxler
